subject: Chemistry
title: Aristotle vs. Atoms
description: A 60-second lesson on Aristotle vs. Atoms.

hook.text: Aristotle argued against atoms. He claimed matter is continuous and made of four elements—earth, water, air, fire.

buildup.text: He believed a vacuum (empty space between atoms) was impossible. 'Nature abhors a vacuum,' he declared.

discovery.text: His four-element system explained observations: wood burns (contains fire), ice melts (contains water).

twist.text: Aristotle's framework was intuitive and wrong. But it dominated science until the 1600s—a 2,000-year detour.

climax.text: The irony: Aristotle championed observation and logic, but his authority prevented others from questioning him.

punchline.text: Intuitive, logical, completely wrong. Authority can block truth for millennia.

quiz.question: What was Aristotle's main argument against atoms?
options: He believed matter was continuous and a vacuum between atoms was impossible
He thought atoms were too small to affect observable properties
He argued that only gods could create indivisible particles
